cluster_name: Trump conditions US-Saudi civilian nuclear deal on Abraham Accords
tldr: The headline layer is almost entirely unified: every outlet except Qatar's Al Jazeera frames this story as Trump imposing an Israel-normalization condition on the US-Saudi nuclear deal, with no space in any headline for the Palestinian statehood counter-narrative or nuclear proliferation risks visible in body text. The main differentiation is tonal — neutral-factual registers ('contingent on,' 'subject to,' 'depends on') predominate across non-Western and establishment outlets, while wire services and several US outlets deploy dramatizing verbs ('stuns,' 'ultimatum,' 'slams brakes,' 'throws into turmoil') that sharpen the surprise-and-disruption frame at the homepage layer.
event_summary: One day after signing a civilian nuclear cooperation agreement with Saudi Arabia, President Trump declared on July 23, 2026 that the deal requires Saudi Arabia to join the Abraham Accords and recognize Israel — a condition absent from the original announcement.

frames.sub_frame: Nuclear deal contingent on Israel ties

evidence.quote: Saudis must recognise Israel for nuclear deal
Trump ties Saudi nuclear deal to Israel normalization
Saudi nuclear deal off without Israel recognition
Trump stuns Saudis by pinning nuclear deal
Trump slams brakes on Saudi nuclear deal

silences.what_they_covered_instead: Al Jazeera's sole headline ('What are the Abraham Accords?') is a pure explainer — it neither reports Trump's condition nor frames the news event, signalling that its audience is expected to be unfamiliar with the normalization framework rather than following the breaking development.

single_outlet_findings.finding: AFP used 'stuns' — unusually dramatized language for a neutral wire service — elevating the surprise element above the factual diplomatic condition; downstream outlets picking up the wire absorb this framing choice.
Only outlet to label Trump's announcement an 'Ultimatum' — the coercive framing is stronger than any other headline in the corpus, casting the condition as a demand rather than a diplomatic linkage.

Reproduces Trump's own evaluative label ('Very Respected' Abraham Accords) inside the headline without editorial distance — the only title in the corpus to carry Trump's self-assessment as headline text rather than a clearly attributed quote.

'Slams brakes' is the most vivid disruption metaphor among US-establishment outlets — treating the deal as practically derailed rather than subject to ongoing negotiation, a register absent from any non-US headline.

'Deal off without Israel recognition' is the most categorical headline in the corpus — binary and absolute where almost every other outlet used conditional language ('contingent on,' 'subject to,' 'hinges on').

Opted for an explainer headline ('What are the Abraham Accords?') rather than reporting Trump's condition — the only outlet to treat the normalization framework itself as the news, implying its audience needs the concept explained rather than the latest development.

bottom_line: At the headline layer, coverage converges on a single fact — Trump conditionally linked the Saudi nuclear deal to Israel recognition — while a fault line in verb choice separates dramatizing US and wire-service outlets ('stuns,' 'ultimatum,' 'slams brakes,' 'throws into turmoil') from neutral-factual non-Western outlets ('contingent on,' 'subject to,' 'depends on'), and the Palestinian statehood obstacle that dominates every body text is entirely absent from the headline layer.
